clinical_history: This ECG from an elderly woman with multiple risk factors for coronary  artery disease, status post anterior MI, shows which ONE of the following rhythms?
diagnosis:
The rhythm is wandering atrial pacemaker (WAP). Note the variable P waves (3 or more morphologies/axes) and PR intervals, with a slightly slow to average rate over 10 seconds of about 67/min (range 58-75bpm). Left axis deviation is present with a QRS axis of about -40 degrees, borderline for left anterior fascicular block. Some of the P waves are broad and notched, with a prominent negative component in lead V1, consistent with left atrial abnormality. The QRS voltage is borderline (lead aVL) for LVH. R wave progression in V3-V6 is very slow, with anterior-lateral T wave inversions consistent with ischemia/MI. 

Clinical correlation confirmed extensive coronary artery disease, status post anterior MI, with a left ventricular ejection fraction by echocardiography of 40%, associated with marked regional anterior wall motion abnormalities. 

WAP can be seen in a variety of settings. Especially at slower rates due to augmented vagal tone (e.g., during deep sleep), it may be a physiologic variant. More gradual variations in P wave appearance are usually seen in the physiologic context. Here WAP is likely a subtle marker of sinus node and atrial electrical dysfunction, but it does not require any specific therapeutic response. 

An alternative answer in this case would be sinus rhythm with multifocal atrial ectopy.  But this type of multifocal rhythm is not MAT, which requires a rapid rate by definition. Physiologic respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA), not seen here (see Case #300), is associated with increases and decreases in heart rate synchronized with inspiration and expiration, respectively, and is a marker of intact vagal cardiac modulation.
